text: The surgeon pushes the gallbladder fundus towards the patient's right shoulder while applying entromedial traction on the harassment pouch.
The ruviate sulcus is visible in the surgical field, providing a landmark during the procedure.
The surgeon separates the posterior peritoneum above the ruviate sulcus, using careful dissection techniques to delineate the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel, an ultrasonic surgical instrument, to perform tissue dissection during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the Teflon jaw of the harmonic device towards the cystic pedicle to prevent vibrations from affecting the gallbladder during dissection.
The surgeon focuses on separating the pectorium, ensuring careful dissection above the ruviate sulcus while maintaining traction on the gallbladder fundus.
The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to open the anterior peritoneum above the roviate sulcus, at the junction of the gallbladder body with the cystic pedicle.
The surgeon begins to skeletonize the cystic pedicle, carefully removing surrounding tissue to expose the anatomical structures within the area.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to enlarge the posterior window by opening its jaws, facilitating further dissection around the cystic pedicle.
The surgeon creates an interior window between the cystic duct and artery, carefully separating these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery, securing it with surgical clips to prevent bleeding during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies three clips to secure the cystic duct and artery, ensuring proper occlusion before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon places two clips towards the right hepatic artery and one clip towards the gallbladder, securing these structures during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon cuts the artery between the second and third clip using surgical scissors, following the placement of clips on the artery.
The surgeon clips the cystic duct, with the option to ligate it, following the clipping of the arteries.
The surgeon rotates the instrument slightly, forms a ring around the structure, and fully clips it, securing the tissue.
The surgeon measures the distance between surgical clips on the duct, with a three-millimeter gap between the first and second clip, and a six-millimeter gap between the second and third clip.
The surgeon cuts the cystic duct after ensuring it is securely clipped or ligated, using surgical scissors to sever the duct.
The surgeon separates the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the liver, completing the dissection between these two structures.
The surgeon applies entro medial and entro lateral traction to the gallbladder, facilitating its separation from the liver bed.
The surgeon slowly separates the gallbladder from the liver bed, continuing the dissection process to detach the remaining tissue connections.
The surgeon begins by separating the peritoneum of the gallbladder to establish a clear dissection plane.

The light cable of the laparoscopic telescope is adjusted to improve visibility, allowing the surgeon to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed. Care is taken during this separation to maintain a clear angular plane.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the harmonic scalpel at the proper angular plane to avoid puncturing the gallbladder while separating it from the liver. As the last pedicle is reached, the light cable is adjusted to inspect for any active bleeding or bile leaks.
The surgeon observes minor oozing as the gallbladder is separated from the liver bed, ensuring the tissue planes are adequately divided.
The surgeon uses a cloth to apply soap, holds the gallbladder at the neck, and maneuvers it into the cannula, preparing to bring all components together for further manipulation.
The surgeon uses suction to remove bile from the surgical field or employs a tool to extract stones from the gallbladder.
